text: In 100m and 200m events, elite runners use an 'all-out' pacing strategy characterized by initial rapid acceleration followed by speed reduction in later stages.
Elite 400m runners display a positive pacing pattern with speed reduction throughout the race.
Elite 800m runners typically adopt positive pacing in meet races but may use positive or negative pacing in championship races depending on tactical approaches of dominant athletes.
A U-shaped pacing strategy (faster start and end than the middle) is evident during world record performances in meet races at 1500m, 5000m, and 10,000m distances.
In championship 5000m and 10,000m races, elite runners adopt a negative pacing strategy with microvariations of pace, while championship 1500m races show a parabolic J-shaped profile.
Recent world record marathon performances are characterized by even pacing, in contrast to major championship marathons which feature positive pacing.
In major championship 1500m and 5000m races, staying within leading positions during the last lap optimizes finishing position.
Athletes with more modest aims than winning medals should adopt a realistic initial pace in long-distance races and stay within a pack of runners.
